content:
# Numbers Chapter 8 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 157–158; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 118–119 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_008_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_008_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_008_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_008_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — FROM DEDICATION TO ILLUMINATION AND CONSECRATION:** Numbers 8 transitions directly from the twelve-day altar dedication of chapter 7 to two related acts of sacred preparation: the arrangement of the lampstand (vv. 1–4) and the purification and consecration of the Levites (vv. 5–26). In the Leningrad Codex, this chapter begins on page 157 (the same page where chapter 7 concludes with the voice from the mercy seat) and continues onto page 158. The chapter divides into two major sections: first, YHWH instructs Aaron on the proper arrangement of the seven lamps so that they cast their light toward the face of the lampstand — a brief but symbolically rich passage connecting the tribal leaders' gold incense spoons to the ongoing luminous service of the tabernacle. Second, the far longer Levite consecration section details a unique purification ritual involving water of sin-purification, full-body shaving, washing of garments, laying on of hands by the Sons of Israel, and a wave-offering presentation before YHWH. The Levites are explicitly designated as a substitute for every firstborn in Israel — a theological claim rooted in the Exodus narrative. The Vaticanus LXX (pp. 118–119) follows the MT closely throughout, with the chapter visible beginning in the third column of pa_0118 (following the end of Numbers 7) and continuing through pa_0119.

---

## The Arrangement of the Lamps (vv. 1–4)

**1** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**2** "Speak [dabber] unto [el] Aaron [Aharon] and-you-shall-say [ve-amarta] unto [el] him: 'In-your-causing-to-go-up [be-ha'alotekha] [et] the-lamps [ha-nerot], toward [el] the-face of [mul penei] the-lampstand [ha-menorah] they-shall-give-light [ya'iru], seven of [shiv'at] the-lamps [ha-nerot].'"

> **Key terms:** בְּהַעֲלֹתְךָ (be-ha'alotekha) = "in your causing to go up" — the hiphil infinitive construct of עלה (alah, "to go up") with a second-person suffix. The lamps are not merely "lit" but "caused to ascend" — the flame is raised upon the wick. This technical term for lamp-lighting recurs throughout the priestly literature (Exod 27:20; 30:8; Lev 24:2). This word gives the parashah (Torah portion) its name: בְּהַעֲלֹתְךָ (Beha'alotekha). אֶל מוּל פְּנֵי הַמְּנוֹרָה (el mul penei ha-menorah) = "toward the face of the lampstand" — the seven lamps are to cast their light forward, toward the central shaft of the menorah, so that the illumination converges inward. Rashi (following Sifrei) explains: the three lamps on the right and the three on the left were angled so their wicks faced the central lamp. **LXX:** ὅταν ἐπιτιθῇς τοὺς λύχνους, ἐκ μέρους κατὰ πρόσωπον τῆς λυχνίας φωτιοῦσιν οἱ ἑπτὰ λύχνοι ("whenever you set up the lamps, from the side toward the face of the lampstand the seven lamps shall give light").

**3** And-did [va-ya'as] so [ken] Aaron [Aharon]: toward [el] the-face of [mul penei] the-lampstand [ha-menorah] he-caused-to-go-up [he'elah] its-lamps [neroteiha], as-which [ka'asher] YHWH commanded [tsivvah] [et] Moses [Mosheh].

> **Key terms:** The obedience formula — וַיַּעַשׂ כֵּן אַהֲרֹן (va-ya'as ken Aharon, "and Aaron did so") — is significant. Rashi comments: "This is stated to tell the praise of Aaron — that he did not deviate." The seemingly obvious compliance note is a literary signal that Aaron performed the lamp service exactly as commanded, without alteration. The phrase כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה (ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et Mosheh) = "as YHWH commanded Moses" — the standard confirmation formula of priestly obedience. **LXX:** καὶ ἐποίησεν οὕτως Ααρων ("and Aaron did thus").

**4** And-this [ve-zeh] [was] the-work of [ma'aseh] the-lampstand [ha-menorah]: hammered-work of [miqshah] gold [zahav] — unto [ad] its-base [yerekah], unto [ad] its-flowers [pirkhah], hammered-work [miqshah] it [hi'] — according-to-the-pattern [ka-mar'eh] which [asher] YHWH showed [her'ah] [et] Moses [Mosheh], so [ken] he-made [asah] [et] the-lampstand [ha-menorah].

> **Key terms:** מִקְשָׁה (miqshah) = "hammered work" — from the root נקשׁ or קשׁה, referring to metal beaten from a single piece (repoussé technique), not assembled from separate cast parts. The entire menorah — base (יָרֵךְ, yarekh, literally "thigh/shaft"), branches, cups, knobs, and flowers (פֶּרַח, perakh) — was formed from one talent of gold by hammering (Exod 25:31–36). כַּמַּרְאֶה (ka-mar'eh) = "according to the appearance/pattern" — referring to the vision or model YHWH showed Moses on the mountain (Exod 25:40). This verse recapitulates Exodus 25:31–40, confirming that the menorah was made exactly according to the divine blueprint. **LXX:** στερεὰ χρυσῆ ("solid gold") for מִקְשָׁה זָהָב — interpreting "hammered-work" as "solid [piece]"; κατὰ τὸ εἶδος ὃ ἔδειξεν κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ ("according to the form which [the] Lord showed to Moses").

---

## The Purification and Consecration of the Levites (vv. 5–22)

**5** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**6** "Take [qakh] [et] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] from-the-midst of [mi-tokh] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-you-shall-purify [ve-tiharta] [et]-them [otam].

> **Key terms:** וְטִהַרְתָּ (ve-tiharta) = "and you shall purify/cleanse them" — the piel perfect of טהר (taher, "to be clean/pure") with consecutive vav, here meaning "to ritually cleanse." The Levites are not merely appointed but must undergo a specific purification process detailed in the following verses. This is distinct from the priestly consecration of Aaron and his sons (Lev 8), which involved anointing oil and sacrificial blood — the Levites receive a lesser but still significant purification. **LXX:** καὶ ἀφαγνιεῖς αὐτούς ("and you shall purify them") — ἀφαγνίζω is a rare verb of ritual cleansing.

**7** And-thus [ve-khoh] you-shall-do [ta'aseh] to-them [lahem] to-purify-them [le-taharatam]: sprinkle [hazzeh] upon-them [alehem] water of [mei] sin-purification [khattat], and-they-shall-cause-to-pass [ve-he'eviru] a-razor [ta'ar] over [al] all [kol] their-flesh [besaram], and-they-shall-wash [ve-khibbesu] their-garments [bigdeihem], and-they-shall-purify-themselves [ve-hittahharu].

> **Key terms:** מֵי חַטָּאת (mei khattat) = "water of sin-purification" — this likely refers to the water mixed with the ashes of the red heifer (Num 19:9, 17–19), though Numbers 19 has not yet been narrated. The term חַטָּאת here carries the sense of "purification from sin/impurity" rather than "sin offering." תַּעַר (ta'ar) = "razor" — the Levites must shave their entire body, a thorough act of purification paralleling the cleansing of a leper (Lev 14:8–9). The three-stage process — sprinkling, shaving, laundering — represents a comprehensive removal of impurity from person, body, and garment. **LXX:** περιρρανεῖς αὐτοὺς ὕδωρ ἁγνισμοῦ ("you shall sprinkle upon them water of purification") — ἁγνισμός for חַטָּאת, emphasizing purification rather than sin.

**8** And-they-shall-take [ve-laqkhu] a-young-bull [par], a-son of [ben] cattle [baqar], and-its-grain-offering [u-minkhato]: fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shamen]; and-a-young-bull [u-far], a-son of [ben] cattle [baqar], a-second [sheni], you-shall-take [tiqqakh] for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat].

> **Key terms:** Two bulls are required: one for a burnt offering (with its accompanying grain offering of fine flour mixed with oil) and one for a sin offering. The double-bull requirement underscores the gravity of the Levites' consecration — they are being set apart for continuous sacred service. The parallel to Aaron's consecration (Lev 8:2, 14) is deliberate: Aaron received a bull for sin offering and a ram for burnt offering; the Levites receive two bulls. **LXX:** καὶ λήμψονται μόσχον ἕνα ἐκ βοῶν καὶ τούτου θυσίαν σεμίδαλιν ἀναπεποιημένην ἐν ἐλαίῳ, καὶ μόσχον ἐνιαύσιον ἐκ βοῶν λήμψῃ περὶ ἁμαρτίας ("and they shall take one calf from the cattle and its sacrifice of fine flour mixed with oil, and a yearling calf from the cattle you shall take for sin").

**9** And-you-shall-bring-near [ve-hiqravta] [et] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] before [lifnei] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed], and-you-shall-assemble [ve-hiqhalta] [et] all [kol] the-congregation of [adat] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** וְהִקְהַלְתָּ (ve-hiqhalta) = "and you shall assemble" — the hiphil of קהל (qahal, "to assemble"), a convocation of the entire community. The Levite consecration is a public act: all Israel must witness and participate (as vv. 10–11 will specify). The entire congregation's presence is required because the Levites serve as substitutes for the firstborn of all Israel. **LXX:** καὶ συνάξεις πᾶσαν συναγωγὴν υἱῶν Ισραηλ ("and you shall gather the whole congregation of the Sons of Israel").

**10** And-you-shall-bring-near [ve-hiqravta] [et] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] before [lifnei] YHWH, and-shall-lay [ve-samekhu] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] [et] their-hands [yedeihem] upon [al] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim].

> **Key terms:** וְסָמְכוּ ... אֶת יְדֵיהֶם (ve-samekhu ... et yedeihem) = "and they shall lay their hands" — the סְמִיכָה (semikhah, "laying on/leaning") of hands is a ritual act of identification and transference. Normally performed by an individual upon a sacrificial animal (Lev 1:4; 3:2), here it is performed by the entire community upon the Levites. The gesture signifies that all Israel transfers its firstborn obligation to the Levites — the Levites will serve in the sanctuary on behalf of the whole people. This is the only instance in the Torah where the laying on of hands is performed upon persons rather than animals. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπιθήσουσιν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ τὰς χεῖρας αὐτῶν ἐπὶ τοὺς Λευίτας ("and the Sons of Israel shall place their hands upon the Levites").

**11** And-shall-wave [ve-henif] Aaron [Aharon] [et] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] [as] a-wave-offering [tenufah] before [lifnei] YHWH from-with [me-et] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-they-shall-be [ve-hayu] for-doing [la-avod] [et] the-service of [avodat] YHWH.

> **Key terms:** וְהֵנִיף אַהֲרֹן אֶת הַלְוִיִּם תְּנוּפָה (ve-henif Aharon et ha-Leviyyim tenufah) = "and Aaron shall wave the Levites [as] a wave-offering" — the hiphil of נוף (nuf, "to wave/elevate"). The תְּנוּפָה (tenufah, "wave offering") was normally a ritual gesture performed with portions of sacrificial meat or grain (Exod 29:24; Lev 7:30). Its application to the Levites is extraordinary: the entire tribe is symbolically "waved" — presented and dedicated — before YHWH. The gesture likely involved the Levites moving forward and back (or being led in procession) rather than physical lifting. מֵאֵת (me-et) = "from with" — the Levites come from among the Sons of Israel, given by the people to YHWH. **LXX:** καὶ ἀφοριεῖ Ααρων τοὺς Λευίτας ἀπόδομα ἔναντι κυρίου ("and Aaron shall set apart the Levites [as] a dedicated gift before [the] Lord") — ἀπόδομα ("a gift given back") for תְּנוּפָה, interpreting the wave offering as a return-gift to God.

**12** And-the-Levites [ve-ha-Leviyyim] shall-lay [yismekhu] [et] their-hands [yedeihem] upon [al] the-head of [rosh] the-young-bulls [ha-parim]; and-make [va-aseh] the-one [et ha-ekhad] [as] a-sin-offering [khattat] and [ve-et] the-one [ha-ekhad] [as] a-burnt-offering [olah] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], to-atone [le-khapper] over [al] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim].

> **Key terms:** Now the Levites themselves perform the hand-laying (סְמִיכָה) upon the bulls' heads — the standard sacrificial identification gesture (Lev 1:4). The two bulls serve different functions: one is a חַטָּאת (khattat, "sin/purification offering") and one is an עֹלָה (olah, "burnt offering/ascension offering"). Together they effect כַּפָּרָה (kapparah, "atonement/covering") for the Levites. The sequence — Israel lays hands on Levites (v. 10), Levites lay hands on bulls (v. 12) — creates a chain of substitutionary identification: Israel -> Levites -> sacrificial animals. **LXX:** ἐξιλάσασθαι περὶ τῶν Λευιτῶν ("to make atonement concerning the Levites").

**13** And-you-shall-stand [ve-ha'amadta] [et] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] before [lifnei] Aaron [Aharon] and-before [ve-lifnei] his-sons [banav], and-you-shall-wave [ve-henafta] [et]-them [otam] [as] a-wave-offering [tenufah] to-YHWH [la-YHWH].

> **Key terms:** The Levites are positioned before Aaron and his sons — the priests to whom the Levites are subordinate in the sanctuary hierarchy. The second mention of the wave offering (תְּנוּפָה) frames the sacrificial action of v. 12: the Levites are waved, then the sacrifices are made, then they are waved again. This double-waving brackets the atoning sacrifice. **LXX:** καὶ στήσεις τοὺς Λευίτας ... καὶ ἀποδώσεις αὐτοὺς ἀπόδομα ἔναντι κυρίου ("and you shall stand the Levites ... and you shall give them back [as] a dedicated gift before [the] Lord").

**14** And-you-shall-separate [ve-hivdalta] [et] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] from-the-midst of [mi-tokh] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-the-Levites [ve-hayu] shall-be [li] mine [ha-Leviyyim].

> **Key terms:** וְהִבְדַּלְתָּ (ve-hivdalta) = "and you shall separate" — the hiphil of בדל (badal, "to separate/distinguish"), the same verb used in Genesis 1 for God's acts of separating light from darkness, waters from waters. The separation of the Levites from Israel mirrors the primordial act of divine ordering: just as God separated elements to create structure, so the Levites are separated to create sacred order within the community. וְהָיוּ לִי הַלְוִיִּם (ve-hayu li ha-Leviyyim) = "and the Levites shall be mine" — the possessive לִי (li, "to me/mine") asserts YHWH's direct ownership of the Levites. **LXX:** καὶ διαστελεῖς τοὺς Λευίτας ("and you shall distinguish the Levites"); καὶ ἔσονταί μοι οἱ Λευῖται ("and the Levites shall be mine").

**15** And-after-that [ve-akharei khen] shall-come [yavo'u] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] to-serve [la-avod] [et] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed]; and-you-shall-purify [ve-tiharta] [et]-them [otam] and-you-shall-wave [ve-henafta] [et]-them [otam] [as] a-wave-offering [tenufah].

> **Key terms:** The sequence is reiterated: purification first, then waving, then service. Only after the full consecration ritual is complete may the Levites begin their work in the Tent of Meeting. The verb עָבַד (avad, "to serve/work") is the characteristic term for Levitical labor — distinct from the priestly כִּהֵן (kihen, "to minister as priest"). **LXX:** καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα εἰσελεύσονται οἱ Λευῖται ἐργάζεσθαι τὰ ἔργα τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("and after these things the Levites shall enter to work the works of the tent of the testimony").

**16** For [ki] given [netunim], given [netunim] they [hemmah] to-me [li] from-the-midst of [mi-tokh] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el]; in-place of [takhat] [the] opening of [piter] every [kol] womb [rekhem], the-firstborn of [bekhor] all [kol] from-the-Sons of [mi-benei] Israel [Yisra'el], I-have-taken [laqakhti] [et]-them [otam] to-me [li].

> **Key terms:** נְתוּנִם נְתוּנִם (netunim netunim) = "given, given" — the emphatic doubling of the passive participle of נתן (natan, "to give") expresses absolute and irrevocable dedication. The Levites are "utterly given" to YHWH. תַּחַת פֶּטֶר כָּל רֶחֶם (takhat piter kol rekhem) = "in place of the opening of every womb" — פֶּטֶר (peter) from פטר (patar, "to open/release"), referring to the firstborn who "opens" the womb. The theological logic: every firstborn in Israel belongs to YHWH (Exod 13:2, 12–15), but the Levites serve as a collective substitute for all the individual firstborn. **LXX:** ἀντὶ παντὸς πρωτοτόκου διανοίγοντος πᾶσαν μήτραν ("in place of every firstborn opening every womb") — the doubling of נְתוּנִם is rendered δόμα δεδομένοι ("a gift, given") in some LXX traditions.

**17** For [ki] to-me [li] [belongs] every [kol] firstborn [bekhor] among-the-Sons of [bi-vnei] Israel [Yisra'el], among-man [ba-adam] and-among-beast [u-va-behemah]; on-the-day [be-yom] I-struck [hakkoti] every [kol] firstborn [bekhor] in-the-land of [be-eretz] Egypt [Mitsrayim], I-sanctified [hiqdashti] them [otam] to-me [li].

> **Key terms:** The grounding of the firstborn claim in the Exodus narrative: YHWH struck (הִכֵּיתִי, hikketi, hiphil of נכה) every firstborn in Egypt and simultaneously sanctified (הִקְדַּשְׁתִּי, hiqdashti, hiphil of קדשׁ) every firstborn in Israel — both human and animal. The tenth plague was both a judgment on Egypt and a consecration of Israel's firstborn. The Levites now bear this consecration on behalf of all the tribes. **LXX:** ᾗ ἡμέρᾳ ἐπάταξα πᾶν πρωτότοκον ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ ἡγίασα αὐτοὺς ἐμοί ("on [the] day I struck every firstborn in [the] land of Egypt, I sanctified them to me").

**18** And-I-took [va-eqqakh] [et] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] in-place of [takhat] every [kol] firstborn [bekhor] among-the-Sons of [bi-vnei] Israel [Yisra'el].

**19** And-I-gave [va-ettenah] [et] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] [as] given-ones [netunim] to-Aaron [le-Aharon] and-to-his-sons [u-le-vanav] from-the-midst of [mi-tokh] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], to-do [la-avod] [et] the-service of [avodat] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] in-the-Tent of [be-Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed], and-to-atone [u-le-khapper] over [al] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el]; and-[there shall] not [ve-lo'] be [yihyeh] among-the-Sons of [bi-vnei] Israel [Yisra'el] a-plague [negef] when-drawing-near [be-geshet] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] unto [el] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]."

> **Key terms:** The Levites serve a dual protective function: (1) לַעֲבֹד אֶת עֲבֹדַת (la-avod et avodat) = "to do the service of" the Sons of Israel — performing the sanctuary labor on Israel's behalf; (2) וּלְכַפֵּר עַל (u-le-khapper al) = "and to atone for" — providing an atonement-covering that shields the people. Without the Levites' intermediary service, the approach of non-consecrated Israelites to the sacred space would result in נֶגֶף (negef, "plague/striking") — divine judgment for breaching the holiness boundary. The phrase בִּגְשֶׁת (bi-geshet, "when drawing near") uses the infinitive construct of נגשׁ (nagash), expressing the dangerous potential of unauthorized approach. **LXX:** καὶ οὐκ ἔσται ἐν τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ προσεγγίζων πρὸς τὰ ἅγια ("and there shall not be among the Sons of Israel [one] drawing near to the holy things") — a slight interpretive expansion.

**20** And-did [va-ya'as] Moses [Mosheh] and-Aaron [ve-Aharon] and-all [ve-khol] the-congregation of [adat] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] to-the-Levites [la-Leviyyim] according-to-all [ke-khol] which [asher] YHWH commanded [tsivvah] [et] Moses [Mosheh] concerning-the-Levites [la-Leviyyim] — so [ken] did [asu] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] to-them [lahem].

> **Key terms:** The comprehensive obedience formula names all three parties who carried out the consecration: Moses (who directed it), Aaron (who performed the priestly acts), and the entire congregation (who laid their hands on the Levites). The double statement of compliance — וַיַּעַשׂ ... כֵּן עָשׂוּ (va-ya'as ... ken asu) — emphasizes that the community performed the ritual exactly as commanded. **LXX:** καὶ ἐποίησεν Μωυσῆς καὶ Ααρων καὶ πᾶσα συναγωγὴ υἱῶν Ισραηλ τοῖς Λευίταις ("and Moses and Aaron and the whole congregation of the Sons of Israel did to the Levites").

**21** And-purified-themselves [va-yitkhatte'u] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] and-they-washed [va-yekhabbsu] [et] their-garments [bigdeihem], and-waved [va-yanef] Aaron [Aharon] [et]-them [otam] [as] a-wave-offering [tenufah] before [lifnei] YHWH; and-atoned [va-yekhapper] Aaron [Aharon] over-them [alehem] to-purify-them [le-taharatam].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּתְחַטְּאוּ (va-yitkhatte'u) = "and they purified themselves" — the hitpa'el of חטא (khata'), here meaning "to de-sin oneself / undergo purification from sin-impurity." This is the reflexive form of the sin/purification root, indicating the Levites' active participation in their own cleansing. The narrative now reports the execution of the ritual prescribed in vv. 7–13: purification, garment-washing, waving, and atonement. **LXX:** καὶ ἡγνίσαντο οἱ Λευῖται ("and the Levites purified themselves"); καὶ ἐξιλάσατο περὶ αὐτῶν Ααρων ("and Aaron made atonement concerning them").

**22** And-after-that [ve-akharei khen] came [ba'u] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim] to-do [la-avod] [et] their-service [avodatam] in-the-Tent of [be-Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed] before [lifnei] Aaron [Aharon] and-before [ve-lifnei] his-sons [banav]; as-which [ka'asher] YHWH commanded [tsivvah] [et] Moses [Mosheh] concerning [al] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim], so [ken] they-did [asu] to-them [lahem].

> **Key terms:** The closing formula of the consecration narrative: the Levites now enter active service, positioned under the authority of Aaron and his sons. The final obedience refrain — כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה עַל הַלְוִיִּם כֵּן עָשׂוּ לָהֶם (ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et Mosheh al ha-Leviyyim ken asu lahem) — seals the section. **LXX:** καθάπερ συνέταξεν κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ περὶ τῶν Λευιτῶν, οὕτως ἐποίησαν αὐτοῖς ("just as [the] Lord commanded Moses concerning the Levites, thus they did to them").

---

## The Age of Levitical Service (vv. 23–26)

**23** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**24** "This [zeh] [is] that-which [asher] [pertains] to-the-Levites [la-Leviyyim]: from-a-son of [mi-ben] twenty [khamesh] and-five [ve-esrim] years [shanah] and-upward [va-ma'lah] he-shall-come [yavo'] to-serve [li-tsevo'] [the] service [tsava'] in-the-service of [ba-avodat] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed].

> **Key terms:** מִבֶּן חָמֵשׁ וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה (mi-ben khamesh ve-esrim shanah) = "from a son of twenty-five years" — the minimum age for Levitical service. This creates an apparent tension with Numbers 4:3, 23, 30, which set the age at thirty. The traditional reconciliation (b. Hullin 24a) holds that the Levites entered a five-year apprenticeship at twenty-five and began full active service at thirty. לִצְבֹא צָבָא (li-tsevo' tsava') = "to serve [the] service" — the cognate accusative construction using צבא (tsava'), which primarily means "army/host/military service" but here denotes the organized workforce of the sanctuary. The military language applied to Levitical labor underscores its disciplined, obligatory character. **LXX:** ἀπὸ πέντε καὶ εἰκοσαετοῦς ... λειτουργεῖν τὰ λειτουργήματα ("from twenty-five years ... to minister the ministries").

**25** And-from-a-son of [u-mi-ben] fifty [khamishim] years [shanah] he-shall-return [yashav] from-the-host of [mi-tseva'] the-service [ha-avodah], and-he-shall-not [ve-lo'] serve [ya'avod] again [od].

> **Key terms:** יָשׁוּב (yashuv) = "he shall return/retire" — the qal of שׁוב (shuv, "to turn/return"), here meaning withdrawal from active duty. At fifty years of age, a Levite retires from the צָבָא (tsava', "host/service-corps"). The retirement age establishes a twenty-five-year career of active sanctuary labor (or twenty years if counting from age thirty per Num 4:3). וְלֹא יַעֲבֹד עוֹד (ve-lo' ya'avod od) = "and he shall not serve again" — a definitive end to his obligatory service, though v. 26 will qualify this. **LXX:** καὶ ἀποστήσεται ἀπὸ τῆς λειτουργίας ("and he shall withdraw from the ministry").

**26** And-he-shall-minister [ve-sheret] with [et] his-brothers [ekhav] in-the-Tent of [be-Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed] to-keep [lishmor] a-charge [mishmeret], and-service [va-avodah] he-shall-not [lo'] serve [ya'avod]. Thus [kakhah] you-shall-do [ta'aseh] to-the-Levites [la-Leviyyim] concerning-their-charges [be-mishmerotam]."

> **Key terms:** וְשֵׁרֵת (ve-sheret) = "and he shall minister" — the piel of שׁרת (sharat, "to minister/attend"), a distinct verb from עבד (avad, "to serve/labor"). The retired Levite may still שׁרת (minister in an assisting, advisory capacity) and לִשְׁמֹר מִשְׁמֶרֶת (lishmor mishmeret, "keep a charge/guard duty"), but he shall not עבד (perform the heavy labor of transport and maintenance). The distinction between שׁרת and עבד is crucial: the aging Levite retains dignity and purpose — he guards, advises, and assists — but is relieved of the physical demands of the primary service. כָּכָה (kakhah) = "thus/in this manner" — a concluding demonstrative that seals the entire chapter's instructions. **LXX:** καὶ λειτουργήσει ὁ ἀδελφὸς αὐτοῦ ἐν τῇ σκηνῇ τοῦ μαρτυρίου φυλάσσειν φυλακάς ("and his brother shall minister in the tent of the testimony to keep watches") — the Vaticanus here appears to read "his brother shall minister" (third person) rather than "he shall minister with his brothers," a notable variant.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **בְּהַעֲלֹתְךָ (be-ha'alotekha) — the ascending flame and the parashah title (v. 2):** The chapter opens with one of the Torah's most evocative technical terms for lamp-lighting. The verb עלה (alah) means "to go up/ascend," and its hiphil causative form הֶעֱלָה (he'elah) means "to cause to ascend." The lamps are not merely "lit" (a word the Hebrew could have expressed with הִדְלִיק, hidliq, or הֵאִיר, he'ir) but "caused to go up" — the flame rises, ascending from the wick like a living thing reaching upward. This parashah (Beha'alotekha, Num 8:1–12:16) takes its name from this word, making the ascending flame the signature image of the entire Torah reading section. The connection between the lamp-lighting and the preceding chapter is liturgically significant: the daily Torah readings for the eight days of Hanukkah conclude with Numbers 7:54–8:4, linking the altar dedication offerings directly to the lighting of the menorah. The lampstand and its lamps appear in the Leningrad Codex on page 157, in the right-hand column, with the characteristic Tiberian vocalization clearly marking the hiphil infinitive construct.

2. **The direction of the lampstand's light — אֶל מוּל פְּנֵי הַמְּנוֹרָה (el mul penei ha-menorah, v. 2):** The instruction that the seven lamps shall give light "toward the face of the lampstand" has generated extensive discussion. The phrase מוּל פְּנֵי (mul penei) means "opposite the face of" or "in front of." Three main interpretations exist: (a) Rashi, following Sifrei (Beha'alotekha 2) and the Talmud (b. Menakhot 98b), explains that the three eastern lamps (on the right branch) and the three western lamps (on the left branch) were angled so their wicks pointed toward the central lamp (the שַׁמָּשׁ, shammash, "servant lamp"), concentrating the light inward; (b) Ibn Ezra understands it as the lamps illuminating the space in front of the menorah — that is, the table of showbread opposite; (c) the Septuagint's ἐκ μέρους κατὰ πρόσωπον τῆς λυχνίας ("from the side toward the face of the lampstand") supports the convergent-light reading. The practical effect in all interpretations is that the lampstand's light is directed, focused, and purposeful — not a scattered glow but a concentrated illumination.

3. **The tension between Numbers 4:3 and 8:24 — age of Levitical service:** Numbers 4:3, 23, and 30 consistently state that the Levitical census counted men "from thirty years old and upward" for service, yet Numbers 8:24 sets the entry age at twenty-five. The Talmud (b. Hullin 24a) resolves this by positing a five-year training period: at twenty-five the Levite begins learning his duties under supervision, and at thirty he enters full active service. First Chronicles 23:24–27 adds another data point: David lowered the service age to twenty, which may reflect a later administrative adjustment when the permanent temple replaced the portable tabernacle and the heavy transport work was no longer needed. The Vaticanus LXX reads ἀπὸ πέντε καὶ εἰκοσαετοῦς (twenty-five) in agreement with the MT in 8:24, confirming that this was not a late textual corruption but an ancient reading. The coexistence of both ages in the canonical text suggests that the distinction between צָבָא-service (the organized labor force, from age twenty-five) and the full עֲבֹדָה (the primary service, from age thirty) may have been a recognized institutional structure.

4. **נְתוּנִם נְתוּנִם (netunim netunim) — the emphatic doubling of "given" (v. 16):** The repetition of the passive participle — "given, given" — is one of the Torah's strongest expressions of irrevocable dedication. The Levites are not conditionally assigned or temporarily loaned; they are absolutely, emphatically given to YHWH. This doubling pattern appears rarely in the Torah (cf. Gen 2:17, מוֹת תָּמוּת, "dying you shall die"; Exod 21:28, סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל, "stoning he shall be stoned") and always signals an intensification that brooks no exception. The theological weight is clear: the Levites' service is not a career they may leave but an identity they bear. The LXX tradition renders this with the doubled form δόμα δεδομένοι in some manuscripts, preserving the emphatic force.

5. **The chain of substitution — hands upon hands upon heads (vv. 10–12):** The Levite consecration ritual creates a unique three-tiered chain of identification: (a) All Israel lays hands on the Levites (v. 10), transferring the firstborn obligation; (b) the Levites lay hands on the bulls' heads (v. 12), transferring their own need for atonement; (c) Aaron offers the bulls as sin offering and burnt offering (v. 12), effecting the actual atonement. This is the only passage in the Torah where humans are the recipients of the סְמִיכָה (semikhah, hand-laying) gesture that is otherwise reserved for sacrificial animals. The theological implication is striking: the Levites occupy a mediating position between the people and the sacrifice, just as they will occupy a mediating position between the people and the holy place. The Vaticanus text on pa_0118–0119 preserves this passage clearly, with the characteristic scriptio continua making the repeated ἐπιθήσουσιν τὰς χεῖρας ("they shall place the hands") visible in the second and third columns.

6. **שֵׁרֵת versus עָבַד — ministry versus labor (v. 26):** The final verse of the chapter introduces a careful distinction between two types of sacred work. The retired Levite (over fifty) may שׁרת (sharat, "minister/attend") but may not עבד (avad, "labor/serve"). In priestly vocabulary, שׁרת typically denotes personal attendance or assisting service — the kind of work that requires wisdom and experience rather than physical strength. עבד denotes the heavy, organized labor of the sanctuary: dismantling, transporting, and re-erecting the tabernacle structure. The distinction preserves the dignity of the aging Levite: he is not dismissed from the sacred community but reassigned to a role that values his accumulated knowledge. The phrase לִשְׁמֹר מִשְׁמֶרֶת (lishmor mishmeret, "to keep a charge") suggests guard duty or custodial oversight — watching over the sacred precincts and instructing younger Levites. The Vaticanus LXX appears to read this verse with a variant subject ("his brother shall minister" rather than "he shall minister with his brothers"), which may reflect a different understanding of the retiree's role.

7. **The structural placement of Numbers 8 — lampstand and Levites as preparation for departure:** Numbers 8 forms the second unit in a carefully ordered sequence preparing Israel for departure from Sinai (which occurs in Num 10:11–12). The sequence is: (a) Num 7: altar dedication (the sacrificial system is inaugurated); (b) Num 8:1–4: lampstand arrangement (the sanctuary's ongoing illumination is established); (c) Num 8:5–26: Levite consecration (the sanctuary's workforce is consecrated); (d) Num 9:1–14: the second Passover (the annual commemorative calendar is set); (e) Num 9:15–23: the cloud and fire guidance system (the travel protocol is established); (f) Num 10:1–10: the silver trumpets (the signal system is established). Only when all these systems are in place — sacrificial, luminous, personnel, calendrical, navigational, and communicative — does Israel break camp and march. Numbers 8's twin concerns (light and Levites) ensure that the tabernacle will function properly on the journey.

8. **LXX Vaticanus distinctive readings (pp. 118–119):** The Vaticanus codex begins Numbers 8 in the third column of pa_0118, following the conclusion of chapter 7 (the voice from the mercy seat). Notable LXX variants in this chapter include: (a) ἀπόδομα ("dedicated gift/return-gift") for תְּנוּפָה ("wave offering") in vv. 11, 13, 15, 21 — the LXX translator understood the Levites' presentation not as a "waving" gesture but as a "giving back" to God, emphasizing that the Levites, who came from among Israel, are returned to their divine owner; (b) ἁγνισμός ("purification") for חַטָּאת ("sin/sin-offering") in v. 7, softening the sin-language toward purification-language; (c) the apparent variant in v. 26 noted above, where the subject of the ministering clause may differ; (d) the Vaticanus consistently uses Λευῖται (Leuitai) in the nominative, preserving the tribal name as a proper designation rather than a functional title. The chapter transition from 7 to 8 is marked in the Vaticanus margin with the characteristic chapter notation visible at the column break on pa_0118.
